rationales.A: Continuing valproate is wrong because it is highly teratogenic (NTD, cardiac, craniofacial, cognitive impairment) and is contraindicated in women of reproductive age unless a TGA Pregnancy Prevention Programme is in place AND no alternative controls seizures; folate alone does not mitigate the teratogenic risk. Would be acceptable only when every alternative antiepileptic has failed and PPP criteria are fully met, never as routine preconception advice (RANZCOG; TGA valproate PPP; AMH).
rationales.C: Abrupt cessation is wrong because antiepileptic withdrawal risks status epilepticus, which carries higher maternal and foetal mortality than the drug itself; the anti-pattern is patient-driven cessation without neurology cover. Would be correct only inside a supervised neurology switch with overlap onto a safer agent, not as standalone advice (RANZCOG; AMH).
rationales.D: Adding lamotrigine on top of valproate is wrong because polytherapy multiplies teratogenic risk rather than reducing it, and valproate must be removed entirely whenever possible. Would be correct only as a transient bridge during a planned cross-titration off valproate under neurology, not as a maintenance plan for pregnancy (RANZCOG; AMH).
rationales.E: Folic acid 400 mcg is wrong because women on antiepileptics require the high-dose 5 mg regimen preconception until 12 weeks, and even adequate folate does not neutralise valproate teratogenicity. Would be correct as the dose for a low-risk woman not on antiepileptics; here both the dose and the drug choice need to change (RANZCOG; AMH).

rationales.B: Maternal antenatal oral vitamin K is wrong because the NHMRC Joint Statement and RANZCOG no longer recommend it for women on antiepileptic drugs; the evidence does not show a clinically meaningful reduction in neonatal haemorrhagic disease over standard neonatal IM vitamin K. Would be correct under the older RCOG-era practice that has now been superseded in Australia (RANZCOG; NHMRC Joint statement on vitamin K).
rationales.C: Switching valproate to lamotrigine at 34 weeks is wrong because organogenesis is complete and a third-trimester swap risks breakthrough seizures with no foetal benefit; the anti-pattern is delayed cross-titration once pregnancy is established. Would be correct only when planned preconception, not as a strategy to reduce neonatal bleeding (Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology).
rationales.D: Elective caesarean is wrong because mode of delivery does not alter the pathophysiology of vitamin K deficient bleeding, which is driven by transplacental enzyme induction by the antiepileptic. Would be appropriate only for standard obstetric indications, never as VKDB prophylaxis (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: FFP prophylaxis is wrong because it is reserved for neonates with active bleeding plus documented coagulopathy, carries transfusion risk, and is never given prophylactically. Would be correct only if the infant developed clinical haemorrhage with deranged coagulation despite vitamin K (Royal Women's Hospital neonatal guidelines).

rationales.A: Continuing the same ICS dose is wrong because deteriorating control (reliever more than twice weekly, nocturnal symptoms, falling FEV1) defines uncontrolled asthma, which is more dangerous in pregnancy than dose escalation; the anti-pattern is therapeutic inertia in the second trimester. Would be correct only if control were stable and symptoms minimal, which is not the case here (RANZCOG; TSANZ; Australian Asthma Handbook).
rationales.C: Stopping ICS is wrong because pregnancy is the exact setting where the Australian Asthma Handbook insists ICS be continued; cessation is associated with exacerbations, pre-eclampsia, prematurity and low birth weight. Would never be correct as a planned change in pregnancy (TSANZ; Australian Asthma Handbook).
rationales.D: Oral prednisolone as maintenance is wrong because oral corticosteroids are reserved for exacerbations, not chronic baseline therapy, due to maternal glycaemic, hypertensive and bone effects; the step-up ladder goes through inhaled ICS-LABA first. Would be correct only if she had severe steroid-dependent asthma already on optimised inhaled therapy (TSANZ; Australian Asthma Handbook).
rationales.E: Montelukast monotherapy is wrong because leukotriene receptor antagonists are an add-on, not a substitute for ICS, in the GINA/TSANZ pathway. Would be correct as a continuation if she was already on montelukast preconception with good control, not as a first-line switch (TSANZ; Australian Asthma Handbook).

rationales.A: Oral montelukast loading is wrong because LTRAs have no acute bronchodilator effect and onset is hours to days; they are chronic add-on agents, not rescue therapy. Would be correct only as part of chronic step-up after discharge, not in acute severe asthma (National Asthma Council Australia).
rationales.B: IV aminophylline is wrong because of its narrow therapeutic window, arrhythmogenic risk and lack of mortality benefit over magnesium; current Australian practice reserves it for refractory cases after IV magnesium has failed. Would be correct only when IV magnesium has been given and the patient is still deteriorating in ICU, not as the next step (Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory).
rationales.C: IM adrenaline is wrong because it is anaphylaxis therapy, and in pregnancy uteroplacental vasoconstriction is a specific concern; the anti-pattern is reaching for adrenaline when the diagnosis is severe asthma not anaphylaxis. Would be correct only if anaphylaxis were the actual mechanism (ASCIA; National Asthma Council Australia).
rationales.D: Emergency caesarean is wrong because delivery is not a treatment for maternal asthma and the foetus is currently reassured; surgery and intubation would worsen bronchospasm. Would be correct only if maternal arrest were imminent or peri-mortem caesarean criteria were met, not at this stage (RANZCOG; Royal Women's Hospital).

rationales.A: Maintaining 100 mcg is wrong because thyroid hormone requirements rise 25-50% in the first trimester driven by hCG-stimulated TBG; delay risks maternal hypothyroidism with neurodevelopmental impact on the foetus, and the Endocrine Society recommends an empirical bump on confirmation of pregnancy. Would be correct only if pre-pregnancy TSH was already optimised at the lower end and a recheck-only strategy was agreed, which is not standard (RANZCOG; Endocrine Society).
rationales.B: Stopping iodine is wrong because NHMRC recommends 150 mcg iodine for all Australian pregnant and lactating women regardless of thyroxine status; iodine supports placental and foetal thyroid synthesis, while levothyroxine replaces maternal hormone. Would never be correct in an Australian antenatal context (NHMRC).
rationales.C: Doubling to 200 mcg is wrong because the evidence-based empirical increment is 20-30% (typically two extra tablets per week); doubling risks iatrogenic thyrotoxicosis with atrial fibrillation and growth restriction. Would be correct only in newly diagnosed overt hypothyroidism with very high TSH at booking, not for stable preconception disease (Endocrine Society).
rationales.D: Switching to liothyronine is wrong because T3 crosses the placenta poorly and the foetus depends on maternal T4 for cerebral development; combination or T3-only therapy is explicitly discouraged in pregnancy. Would be correct only in extremely rare specialist scenarios, never in routine antenatal care (RANZCOG; Endocrine Society).

rationales.A: Continuing carbimazole in the first trimester is wrong because it is associated with embryopathy (aplasia cutis, choanal and oesophageal atresia, omphalocoele) during organogenesis; the standard carve-out is PTU until 16 weeks then switch back. Would be correct only after the first trimester is complete, not from 6 weeks (RANZCOG; Endocrine Society).
rationales.B: Radioactive iodine ablation is wrong because it is an absolute contraindication in pregnancy due to foetal thyroid ablation and teratogenic radiation exposure. Would be correct only postpartum after cessation of breastfeeding with appropriate isolation precautions (Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology).
rationales.C: Stopping all antithyroid drug is wrong because biochemically active Graves disease with raised TRAb risks maternal thyroid storm, foetal goitre and foetal tachycardia; the anti-pattern is treating pregnancy itself as remission. Would be correct only if she were in true biochemical remission with negative TRAb and stable off therapy preconception (Endocrine Society).
rationales.E: Adding propranolol while continuing carbimazole is wrong because beta blockade addresses adrenergic symptoms (which she does not have) without removing the teratogenic exposure during organogenesis. Would be appropriate as a short-term adjunct in symptomatic thyrotoxicosis or thyroid storm, not as a strategy to neutralise carbimazole risk (Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology).

rationales.B: Stopping hydroxychloroquine is wrong because HCQ is the one drug we deliberately keep going through pregnancy in SLE: it reduces flares, reduces recurrence of congenital heart block in subsequent pregnancies, and may reduce pre-eclampsia. Would be correct only if she developed retinopathy or another HCQ-specific adverse effect, not because of the pregnancy itself (EULAR SLE pregnancy guidelines; RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Empirical IVIG is wrong because trials have failed to show that IVIG prevents first-occurrence congenital heart block; the established strategy is foetal echo surveillance with prompt intervention if first-degree block emerges. Would be considered only as research therapy or in selected high-risk recurrence cases at specialist discretion, not as routine primary prevention (EULAR).
rationales.D: Stopping aspirin is wrong because SLE carries a high pre-eclampsia risk and SOMANZ recommends low-dose aspirin from before 16 weeks for this exact indication; anti-Ro status does not change bleeding risk meaningfully. Would be correct only with active bleeding or genuine aspirin allergy (SOMANZ; RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Empirical LMWH is wrong because thromboprophylaxis is reserved for antiphospholipid syndrome, prior VTE or otherwise high VTE risk; SLE alone with negative APL antibodies does not meet RCOG thromboprophylaxis criteria. Would be correct in coexisting APS, not in isolated SLE (RCOG; RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Therapeutic-dose enoxaparin alone is wrong because the obstetric APS regimen pairs prophylactic (not therapeutic) LMWH with aspirin; therapeutic dose is reserved for APS with prior thrombosis or current VTE. Would be correct only if she had previous thrombotic APS, not pure obstetric APS (RCOG Green-top 17; RANZCOG).
rationales.B: Aspirin alone is wrong because aspirin monotherapy has not consistently reduced miscarriage in obstetric APS; the synergistic effect comes from aspirin plus LMWH. Would be correct only for women with isolated APL antibodies and no obstetric APS criteria met, not for true obstetric APS like this case (RCOG Green-top 17).
rationales.C: Warfarin titration is wrong because warfarin is teratogenic from approximately 6 to 12 weeks (warfarin embryopathy with nasal hypoplasia and chondrodysplasia punctata); LMWH is the pregnancy anticoagulant of choice. Would be correct only in mechanical heart valve scenarios with explicit risk-benefit counselling, not in obstetric APS (Therapeutic Guidelines; RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Rivaroxaban is wrong because DOACs cross the placenta, lack safety data and are explicitly contraindicated in pregnancy; the TRAPS trial also showed rivaroxaban inferiority to warfarin in triple-positive APS outside pregnancy. Would never be correct in pregnant obstetric APS (Therapeutic Guidelines; AMH).

rationales.A: Routine elective caesarean is wrong because the ASHM/BHIVA threshold for offering caesarean is viral load at or above 50 copies/mL (or unknown) near term; with sustained suppression, vaginal birth has equivalent transmission risk and lower maternal morbidity. Would be correct only with detectable or undocumented viral load close to delivery (ASHM; BHIVA).
rationales.C: Stopping ART is wrong because viral rebound after cessation is rapid and would directly increase vertical transmission risk in late pregnancy and labour; the anti-pattern is treating ART as optional once viral load is suppressed. Would never be correct in pregnancy (ASHM).
rationales.D: Elective caesarean plus IV zidovudine is wrong because intrapartum IV zidovudine is reserved for viral load at or above 400 copies/mL or unknown status; layering caesarean plus IV zidovudine on a virologically suppressed woman adds morbidity without benefit. Would be correct only if her viral load were not suppressed (BHIVA).
rationales.E: Induction at 37 weeks is wrong because there is no evidence early induction reduces vertical transmission in suppressed women; obstetric indications drive timing of birth, and iatrogenic prematurity carries neonatal morbidity. Would be correct only for standard obstetric reasons, not as HIV-specific intervention (ASHM; RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Triple infant ART is wrong because high-risk neonatal regimens (zidovudine plus lamivudine plus nevirapine or raltegravir) are reserved for infants of mothers with detectable VL near term, late diagnosis or seroconversion in pregnancy; this infant is low risk. Would be correct only if maternal viral load were not suppressed at delivery (BHIVA).
rationales.B: Exclusive breastfeeding is wrong because Australian ASHM guidance recommends formula feeding in high resource settings: residual transmission risk is not zero even on ART, and formula is safe and accessible. Would be the WHO recommendation in resource-limited settings where replacement feeding is not AFASS, not in Australia (ASHM; WHO).
rationales.D: Mixed feeding is wrong because it is the highest-risk feeding pattern: HIV exposure from breast milk combined with mucosal disruption from formula. Would never be correct for HIV-exposed infants (WHO; ASHM).
rationales.E: No infant prophylaxis is wrong because all HIV-exposed infants receive at least short-course zidovudine even with suppressed maternal VL to cover residual transmission risk. Would be correct only if HIV status had been completely excluded antenatally, not after confirmed maternal HIV (ASHM; RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Maternal HBIG is wrong because passive immunoglobulin given to the mother does not reduce viral load and HBIG has no role in maternal antenatal prevention; HBIG belongs to the infant within 12 hours of birth. Would be correct only for non-immune individuals after high-risk exposure, not as antenatal prophylaxis (Australian Immunisation Handbook).
rationales.B: Entecavir is wrong because Australian guidance prefers tenofovir disoproxil in pregnancy due to a much larger safety dataset; entecavir is Pregnancy Category B3 with limited human data. Would be correct only in tenofovir intolerance or resistance, not first-line (ASHM; GESA).
rationales.D: Elective caesarean is wrong because mode of delivery does not change HBV transmission when infant HBIG plus birth-dose vaccine are given; the anti-pattern is using caesarean as a viral barrier the way it works for HIV. Would be appropriate only for standard obstetric indications (ASHM; RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Advising against breastfeeding is wrong because HBIG plus birth-dose vaccine makes breastfeeding safe even with high maternal DNA; the Australian Immunisation Handbook explicitly endorses breastfeeding in HBV-positive women. Would be correct only with cracked nipples and uncovered infant (Australian Immunisation Handbook; ASHM).

rationales.B: Starting DAA at 12 weeks is wrong because sofosbuvir/velpatasvir is not yet recommended outside research protocols in Australian pregnancy due to limited pharmacokinetic and safety data; deferring to postpartum is standard. Would be correct only within a registered trial, not as routine care (ASHM; GESA).
rationales.C: Elective caesarean is wrong because HCV vertical transmission is not reduced by caesarean delivery; intrapartum factors (prolonged ROM, foetal scalp electrodes) matter more than mode of birth. Would be appropriate only for standard obstetric indications (ASHM; RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Advising against breastfeeding is wrong because HCV is not transmitted through intact lactation; ASHM and ABA explicitly recommend breastfeeding with the carve-out to pause feeding from a side with cracked or bleeding nipples. Would be correct only with active nipple trauma on the involved side (ASHM; Australian Breastfeeding Association).
rationales.E: Neonatal anti-HCV at 48 hours is wrong because passively acquired maternal antibody persists in the infant for up to 18 months, making early antibody testing uninformative; HCV PCR at 2 and 6 months or antibody after 18 months is the recommended schedule. Would be correct only for documenting transient maternal antibody, not for diagnosing infant infection (ASHM).

rationales.A: Doing nothing is wrong because >50 x10^9/L is the threshold for vaginal birth without instrumentation, not for neuraxial anaesthesia, which requires >70-80 x10^9/L per ASH 2019 and Australian practice. Would be correct only if she planned vaginal birth without epidural and no operative interventions (ASH 2019; KEMH).
rationales.C: Pre-emptive platelet transfusion is wrong because platelet survival is short in ITP due to antibody-mediated destruction, so transfusion does not provide durable counts; it is reserved for active bleeding or imminent surgery. Would be correct only at the time of caesarean or with bleeding, not as antenatal optimisation (ASH 2019).
rationales.D: Eltrombopag is wrong because TPO receptor agonists are second/third-line in ITP and have limited pregnancy safety data; first-line in pregnancy is prednisolone with IVIG as second-line. Would be correct only in refractory ITP after corticosteroids and IVIG have failed (Therapeutic Guidelines; ASH 2019).
rationales.E: Discussing splenectomy is wrong because the question asks about delivery-time platelet optimisation and splenectomy does not address the current pregnancy; it is also factually misleading - splenectomy remains an option for selected refractory chronic ITP. Would be a postpartum discussion only after standard therapies fail (ASH 2019).

rationales.A: Stopping azathioprine and switching to budesonide is wrong because thiopurines maintain remission whereas budesonide is induction therapy with limited long-term maintenance efficacy; flares in pregnancy harm the foetus more than the drug. Would be correct only if she were intolerant of azathioprine and seeking short-term flare control, not as preconception maintenance (GESA; ECCO).
rationales.C: Stopping adalimumab at conception is wrong because anti-TNF agents are continued at least through to 24-30 weeks (often to delivery, depending on the specific biologic) because flare risk outweighs theoretical immunogenicity; the anti-pattern is reflexive drug holidays. Would be correct only if she were in deep remission with biomarker confirmation and accepted flare risk, and even then most guidelines do not endorse cessation (GESA; ECCO).
rationales.D: Restarting methotrexate is wrong because methotrexate is teratogenic (folate antagonism, NTDs, craniofacial anomalies) and is contraindicated through pregnancy and the 3-6 month preconception window for both partners. Would never be correct during conception attempts or pregnancy (Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology).
rationales.E: Switching to mycophenolate is wrong because MMF is teratogenic (EMFO syndrome - cleft lip/palate, microtia, cardiac, oesophageal) and explicitly contraindicated in pregnancy; pregnancy prevention is required during MMF use. Would be correct only outside pregnancy planning, never as preconception maintenance (Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology).

rationales.A: Substituting metformin for insulin is wrong because metformin does not provide adequate glycaemic control in type 1 diabetes and does not address the absolute insulin deficiency; pre-existing T1DM requires intensive insulin therapy. Would be appropriate only as an adjunct in type 2 diabetes or PCOS, not as substitution in T1DM (ADIPS).
rationales.B: Reassuring about HbA1c 8.4% is wrong because this level corresponds to substantially elevated rates of major congenital anomaly, miscarriage and stillbirth; ADIPS targets <6.5% (or <7.0% if hypoglycaemia limits) before conception attempts. Would be correct only if no further optimisation were achievable and she had been counselled on residual risk, which is not the case here (ADIPS).
rationales.D: Advising against pregnancy is wrong because background retinopathy is not a contraindication; pregnancy is permitted with optimised glycaemia and antenatal ophthalmology surveillance every trimester. Would be correct only for severe untreated proliferative retinopathy or advanced cardiac/renal complications (ADIPS; RANZCOG).
rationales.E: SGLT2 inhibitors are wrong because they precipitate euglycaemic DKA in T1DM, are contraindicated in pregnancy and have no role in preconception glycaemic intensification. Would never be correct in T1DM preconception planning (Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology).

rationales.A: Continuing the pump with 4-hourly checks is wrong because intrapartum T1DM needs hourly capillary glucose to stay in the narrow 4-7 mmol/L band; pumps may continue only with strict hourly monitoring and a written failure protocol. Would be correct only with hourly testing and explicit pump-continuation protocol, not 4-hourly (ADIPS; RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Stopping all insulin is wrong because basal insulin withdrawal in T1DM precipitates DKA within hours; the anti-pattern is treating T1DM like T2DM where insulin can be paused. Would never be correct in T1DM intrapartum (Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology).
rationales.D: Higher targets are wrong because intrapartum maternal hyperglycaemia drives foetal hyperinsulinism and neonatal hypoglycaemia after cord clamp; 4-7 mmol/L is the established target. Would be correct only as a transient ceiling during hypoglycaemia recovery, not as a deliberate target (ADIPS).
rationales.E: Single long-acting glargine bolus is wrong because long-acting analogues are not titratable and cannot match the rapid changes in insulin requirement during labour and immediately postpartum; variable rate IV insulin is preferred. Would be correct only as an outpatient regimen, not in active labour (ADIPS).

rationales.A: LMWH throughout is wrong because mechanical mitral valve thrombosis rates are higher on LMWH-only regimens than on warfarin-containing strategies despite anti-Xa monitoring; ESC reserves this approach for women who decline warfarin after structured counselling. Would be correct only as a patient-preference option after counselling, not as default (ESC 2018).
rationales.B: Warfarin throughout is wrong because it carries warfarin embryopathy risk between 6 and 12 weeks (nasal hypoplasia, chondrodysplasia punctata) and foetal intracranial haemorrhage at delivery; pure-warfarin strategy is acceptable only when daily warfarin dose is low (<5 mg) with explicit counselling. Would be correct only when warfarin dose is <5 mg/day and the woman accepts foetal risk after counselling, not as default for high-dose warfarin requirements (ESC 2018).
rationales.D: Rivaroxaban is wrong because DOACs are contraindicated in pregnancy and ineffective at preventing mechanical valve thrombosis (RE-ALIGN trial showed harm with dabigatran). Would never be correct in mechanical valves, pregnant or not (ESC 2018; AMH).
rationales.E: Stopping anticoagulation in the first trimester is wrong because mechanical mitral valves have very high thrombotic risk and require uninterrupted anticoagulation; periods without anticoagulant cause valve thrombosis and maternal death. Would never be correct (ESC 2018).

rationales.A: Apixaban is wrong because DOACs are not recommended for postpartum thromboprophylaxis in Australia, partly due to limited lactation data and partly because LMWH has stronger evidence; the anti-pattern is extrapolating non-pregnant orthopaedic practice. Would be correct only outside the postpartum/breastfeeding context (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: Mechanical prophylaxis alone is wrong because emergency caesarean plus multiple risk factors crosses the threshold for pharmacological prophylaxis; stockings are an adjunct, not a substitute. Would be correct only when LMWH is contraindicated (e.g. active bleeding) (RCOG Green-top 37a).
rationales.C: Therapeutic-dose LMWH is wrong because therapeutic dosing is for confirmed VTE or very-high-risk prophylaxis (recent VTE, APS with prior thrombosis); the bleeding risk is unjustified for prophylactic indications. Would be correct only if she had had a recent VTE or thrombotic APS (RCOG Green-top 37a).
rationales.E: Aspirin is wrong because aspirin lacks evidence for postnatal VTE prevention in this risk profile and is not recommended by RCOG Green-top 37a; LMWH is the agent of choice. Would be correct only as pre-eclampsia prophylaxis, not VTE (RCOG Green-top 37a).

rationales.B: Emollients alone with 39-40 week delivery is wrong because emollients are purely symptomatic and do not address the underlying cholestasis; the stillbirth curve at bile acids 56 micromol/L rises substantially after 38 weeks, making term expectancy unsafe. Would be correct only for mild ICP (bile acids <40 micromol/L) where 39-40 week delivery is acceptable (RCOG ICP 2022).
rationales.C: Cholestyramine plus immediate delivery is wrong because cholestyramine has fallen out of first-line use due to fat-soluble vitamin malabsorption (including vitamin K) and immediate delivery at 35+2 is reserved for severe disease at bile acids >=100 micromol/L. Would be correct only at severe-disease thresholds, not at 56 micromol/L (RCOG ICP 2022; RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Dexamethasone is wrong because corticosteroids are not effective primary treatment for ICP; UDCA is the established pharmacological agent. Would be correct only for antenatal lung maturation if early preterm delivery were planned, not as ICP therapy (RCOG ICP 2022).
rationales.E: Antihistamines plus 40-week expectancy is wrong because chlorphenamine only modestly improves itch and does not change bile acid kinetics or stillbirth risk; delivery to 40 weeks at this bile acid level exceeds the safety window. Would be correct only for benign pruritus of pregnancy without raised bile acids (RCOG ICP 2022).

rationales.A: Hypertension and proteinuria is wrong because it is more characteristic of HELLP (which sits on the pre-eclampsia spectrum) than AFLP, where blood pressure is often only modestly elevated and proteinuria less prominent. Would point to HELLP/pre-eclampsia rather than AFLP (RANZCOG).
rationales.B: RUQ pain with transaminases >500 is wrong because both AFLP and HELLP can present with this picture; it is shared rather than discriminating. Would be neutral, not diagnostic, between the two (Swansea criteria).
rationales.C: Platelets <100 is wrong because it is part of HELLP criteria and can occur in AFLP from synthetic failure too; in AFLP the platelet drop is usually modest relative to the coagulopathy. Would be a HELLP criterion not a discriminator (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Hyperuricaemia is wrong because raised urate is a classic pre-eclampsia/HELLP marker (placental ischaemia, reduced renal clearance) and is not specific to AFLP. Would point to HELLP/pre-eclampsia (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: MCDA labelling is wrong because the lambda sign is the dichorionic marker; the T-sign (membrane meeting placenta at 90 degrees with no chorion wedge) defines MCDA. Would be correct only if the scan showed a T-sign, not a lambda (RANZCOG twins; ISUOG).
rationales.C: MCMA labelling is wrong because monoamniotic twinning requires a single sac with no inter-twin membrane; the report describes two sacs and a thick separating membrane. Would be correct only with no visible membrane and twins moving in a shared sac (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Saying chorionicity cannot be assigned until 11-14 weeks is wrong because first-trimester assessment is most accurate and the lambda/T sign is reliable from around 7-9 weeks; deferring assessment loses the most accurate window. Would be correct only if first-trimester views are technically inadequate, requiring rescan (ISUOG).
rationales.E: Dichorionic monoamniotic is wrong because it is not a real entity: each chorion brings its own amnion, so two chorions means two amnions. Would never be a valid classification (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Reassurance and fortnightly surveillance is wrong because untreated Quintero stage II TTTS has high perinatal mortality; the anti-pattern is waiting for progression. Would be correct only for Stage I with stable Dopplers under close specialist review, not Stage II (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Amnioreduction is wrong because the Eurofoetus and subsequent trials showed fetoscopic laser ablation gives superior dual-survival and lower neurological morbidity than amnioreduction in stage II-IV TTTS at 16-26 weeks. Would be acceptable only if laser is unavailable, the woman declines transfer, or gestation is outside the laser window (RANZCOG; ISUOG).
rationales.D: Antenatal corticosteroids and delivery at 19+5 is wrong because 19+5 weeks is pre-viable; delivery would result in dual neonatal loss. Would be correct only at periviable gestations after 24 weeks with stage III-IV TTTS refractory to laser (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Cord occlusion of the donor is wrong because selective reduction by cord occlusion is reserved for Stage IV-V or when laser has failed/is not feasible (e.g. severe hydrops in one twin, anterior placenta with poor access); at Stage II laser is first line. Would be correct only as salvage therapy when laser is unsuitable (RANZCOG; ISUOG).

rationales.A: Type I labelling is wrong because Gratacos Type I has persistently positive UA end-diastolic flow with a favourable prognosis; intermittent absent end-diastolic flow defines Type III. Would be correct only if UA Dopplers remained continuously positive (Gratacos sIUGR classification).
rationales.B: Type II labelling is wrong because Type II shows persistently absent or reversed UA flow with progressive deterioration and earlier delivery (often 28-30 weeks); the intermittent pattern here is Type III. Would be correct only if AEDF or REDF were persistent across studies (Gratacos sIUGR classification).
rationales.D: Reassuring as benign discordance is wrong because EFW below the 10th centile in either twin combined with greater than 25% discordance meets the consensus definition of sIUGR; the anti-pattern is treating this as physiological variation. Would be correct only if both twins were AGA with milder discordance, not here (ISUOG).
rationales.E: TTTS labelling is wrong because TTTS requires discordant fluid (DVP <2 cm in one and >8 cm in the other); fluid is normal in both sacs, so the pathology is sIUGR not TTTS. Would be correct only if oligohydramnios-polyhydramnios were present (RANZCOG; ISUOG).

rationales.B: Anti-D 250 IU is wrong because the NBA dose increases to 625 IU IM from 13+0 weeks for sensitising events; 250 IU is the pre-12-week dose. Would be correct only before 13 weeks, not at exactly 13+0 (National Blood Authority Anti-D guidelines).
rationales.C: Withholding anti-D until 28 weeks is wrong because sensitising events require anti-D within 72 hours regardless of negative antibody screen or upcoming routine prophylaxis; waiting risks sensitisation. Would be correct only for a non-sensitising event with no bleeding (National Blood Authority).
rationales.D: Surgical evacuation is wrong because vanishing twins resorb naturally and instrumentation risks loss of the surviving twin from membrane disruption or infection. Would be correct only if there were septic retained products threatening the surviving pregnancy, which is not described (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Serial coagulation studies for DIC is wrong because maternal DIC risk applies to single-twin demise after viability (typically >=20 weeks) with prolonged in-utero retention; first-trimester vanishing twin does not generate the necessary thromboplastin load. Would be correct only for second/third-trimester co-twin demise (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: 38-39 weeks is wrong because MCDA twins are not safely carried that far: shared circulation drives a stillbirth curve that rises sharply after 36+6, and RANZCOG sets the planned-birth window at 36+0 to 36+6. Would be correct only for uncomplicated DCDA twins (RANZCOG twins; KEMH).
rationales.C: Elective caesarean at 36+0 is wrong because chorionicity alone does not mandate caesarean; with twin 1 cephalic and no other contraindications, planned vaginal birth is recommended. Would be correct only if twin 1 were non-cephalic or another caesarean indication existed (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Caesarean at 32+0 is wrong because iatrogenic delivery at 32 weeks imposes major prematurity morbidity (RDS, NEC, IVH) without benefit in an uncomplicated MCDA pregnancy. Would be correct only with severe TTTS/sIUGR or significant maternal-foetal compromise (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: 37+0 caesarean regardless of presentation is wrong on two counts: 37+0 sits beyond the RANZCOG MCDA window and caesarean is not mandated when twin 1 is cephalic. Would be correct only if both twins were past the window and twin 1 was non-cephalic (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: 36+0 to 36+6 is wrong because that is the MCDA window; in MCMA, cord entanglement risk rises after 32 weeks and waiting to 36 is unsafe. Would be correct for MCDA, not MCMA (RANZCOG; KEMH).
rationales.C: Vaginal birth with CTG is wrong because cord entanglement in a shared sac makes labour intrinsically high-risk regardless of presentation; the anti-pattern is treating MCMA like MCDA. Would never be correct in MCMA, even with cephalic-cephalic presentation (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Aiming for 37+0 vaginally is wrong because it stacks the worst of both errors: extending well past the MCMA safety window plus exposing twins to labour-related entanglement risk. Would never be correct in MCMA (RANZCOG; KEMH).
rationales.E: Immediate caesarean at 31+4 is wrong because without a deterioration trigger (abnormal CTG, sIUGR progression, TTTS), birth at 31+4 imposes prematurity morbidity that the 32-34 week window is designed to avoid. Would be correct only with evidence of foetal compromise or maternal indication (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Nifedipine is wrong because Therapeutic Guidelines flag a relative contraindication when co-prescribed with antihypertensives like labetalol due to additive hypotension and risk of uteroplacental compromise. Would be correct only in women not on antihypertensives without cardiovascular concerns (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics).
rationales.C: Indomethacin is wrong because it is generally avoided from around 32 weeks due to foetal ductus arteriosus constriction and oligohydramnios; even though 28+3 is technically within window, atosiban has a better maternal safety profile here. Would be a reasonable alternative under 32 weeks if atosiban is unavailable (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics).
rationales.D: Magnesium sulfate as tocolysis is wrong because magnesium has been shown ineffective as a tocolytic and is instead reserved for foetal neuroprotection at <30 weeks. Would be correct only as neuroprotection (the question separately requires this), not tocolysis (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: IV salbutamol is wrong because beta-agonists are no longer recommended in Australia for tocolysis (maternal tachycardia, pulmonary oedema, hyperglycaemia, hypokalaemia); they have been withdrawn from first-line practice. Would never be first-line in current Australian guidance (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics).

rationales.B: Pulmonary oedema is wrong because that adverse profile belongs to beta-agonists (and prolonged combination tocolysis), not indomethacin; the question targets indomethacin-specific harm at 33+1. Would point to salbutamol or atosiban concerns instead (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics).
rationales.C: Maternal hypotension is wrong because vasodilator hypotension is a calcium channel blocker (nifedipine) effect, not an indomethacin effect; indomethacin acts on prostaglandin synthesis. Would describe nifedipine, not indomethacin (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics).
rationales.D: Prior preterm birth as absolute contraindication is wrong because prior preterm birth is the indication for treatment, not a contraindication; gestational age and renal/GI status are the relevant flags. Would never be a valid contraindication (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics).
rationales.E: Reduced steroid efficacy is wrong because there is no evidence indomethacin attenuates betamethasone lung maturation effect; the late-gestation concern is foetal ductus and oligohydramnios. Would not be a recognised concern (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics).

rationales.A: Giving betamethasone at 36+2 is wrong because RANZCOG limits routine antenatal corticosteroids to 24+0 to 34+6 weeks; routine use beyond 35 weeks does not improve neonatal respiratory outcomes enough to outweigh harms including neonatal hypoglycaemia. Would be correct only within 24+0 to 34+6 with imminent birth (RANZCOG ANC guideline).
rationales.C: Dexamethasone 6 mg q12h x4 is wrong because the RANZCOG dexamethasone regimen is 12 mg IM 24 hours apart for two doses; the option misstates the dosing and gives steroids outside the gestational window. Would never match Australian dosing (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Giving within an hour of birth is wrong because corticosteroids require several hours to upregulate surfactant production; the gestational cutoff is the issue here, not timing within the same hour. Would be a misconception about mechanism (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Rescue dose at 36+2 is wrong because rescue courses are considered before 34+0 weeks when the prior course was over 7 days ago and birth is imminent; 36+2 is outside the window. Would be correct only at <34+0 with prior course >7 days earlier (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Full repeat course is wrong because Australian guidance favours a single rescue dose rather than a full repeat; multiple repeat courses are associated with reduced birth weight and head circumference. Would be correct only in some international protocols, not RANZCOG (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Withholding steroids entirely is wrong because evidence (ACTORDS, MACS analyses) shows a rescue dose provides meaningful neonatal benefit when the prior course was over 7 days ago and birth is now imminent. Would be correct only if the original course were within the past 7 days (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Dexamethasone 6 mg single dose is wrong because Australian dexamethasone dose is 12 mg per dose, and the preferred rescue agent is betamethasone 11.4 mg IM; the dose stated is half-strength. Would never match Australian rescue dosing (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Withholding until >5 cm is wrong because steroid foetal benefit takes hours to develop; the anti-pattern is treating steroid timing as a labour-progress decision rather than an anticipation-of-birth decision. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).
